items.title: Endometriosis as the main cause
items.contentHtml:
Endometriosis

  • Endometriosis produces chronic pelvic pain in 70-80% of those affected; ectopic endometrial implants outside the uterine cavity.
  • Cyclic pain with intense dysmenorrhea that progresses to chronic non-cyclic pain due to fibrosis and adhesions.
  • Definitive diagnosis by diagnostic laparoscopy with direct visualization and biopsy; rASRM classification (score 1-150).
  • First line medical: continuous combined oral contraceptives or progestogens (dienogest 2 mg/day).
  • Trap: partial response to NSAIDs suggests profound endometriosis; Do not rule out by normal ultrasound.

items.title: painful bladder syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Painful bladder syndrome

  • Also called interstitial cystitis; suprapubic pain associated with urinary urgency and frequency without urinary infection.
  • Diagnostic criteria: pelvic pain perceived as bladder pain for >6 weeks, with at least one urinary symptom (urgency or frequency).
  • Finding on cystoscopy: glomeruli (submucosal petechiae after hydrodistension) or Hunner's ulcers (fibrotic red lesions).
  • First line: sodium pentosan polysulfate 300 mg/day (repairs glycosaminoglycan layer) and amitriptyline 25-75 mg/night.
  • Trap: do not confuse with recurrent urinary tract infection; Mandatory negative urine culture.

items.title: Gynecological and non-gynecological causes
items.contentHtml:
Gynecological and non-gynecological causes

  • Gynecological causes: endometriosis, adenomyosis, chronic pelvic inflammatory disease, fibroids, ovarian cysts, pelvic adhesions.
  • Urological causes: interstitial cystitis, ureteral lithiasis, recurrent urinary tract infection, stenosis urethral.
  • Gastrointestinal causes: irritable bowel syndrome, inflammatory bowel disease, chronic diverticulitis.
  • Musculoskeletal causes: pelvic floor dysfunction, myofascial syndrome, pudendal neuralgia.
  • Trap: in >40% two or more etiologies coexist; The differential diagnosis must be systematic and multidisciplinary.

items.title: Pelvic pain of myofascial origin
items.contentHtml:
Pelvic pain of myofascial origin

  • Pain referred from trigger points in pelvic floor muscles (levator ani, obturator internus, piriformis).
  • The pain is reproduced with palpation of the trigger point and follows a characteristic reference pattern (e.g. point in obturator internus radiates to the vagina or rectum).
  • Clinical diagnosis: examination manual pelvic floor looking for tight bands and pain on pressure.
  • First line: pelvic floor physiotherapy with manual release, dry needling or local anesthetic injection (lidocaine 1% 1-2 mL per point).
  • Trap: not to be confused with visceral pain; Myofascial pain worsens with muscle contraction and improves with stretching.

items.title: Adenomyosis and cyclical pain
items.contentHtml:
Adenomyosis and cyclical pain

  • Adenomyosis: presence of ectopic endometrium within the myometrium, causing intense cyclical pain and menorrhagia.
  • Colic-like pain that begins premenstrually and is accentuated during menstruation; uterus enlarged and painful to the touch.
  • Diagnosis by imaging: transvaginal ultrasound shows heterogeneous myometrium with linear hypoechoic areas and zebra sign; MRI confirms with thickened junctional unit >12 mm.
  • First line of medicine: levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine device (LNG-IUD) reduces pain and bleeding.
  • Trap: adenomyosis can coexist with endometriosis; The LNG-IUD is also effective in superficial endometriosis.

items.title: Chronic pelvic inflammatory disease
items.contentHtml:
Chronic pelvic inflammatory disease

  • Sequela of acute pelvic inflammatory disease due to untreated Chlamydia trachomatis or Neisseria gonorrhoeae; generates adhesions and chronic pain.
  • Bilateral, dull pelvic pain, with exacerbation during menstruation or sexual relations; may be associated with tubal infertility.
  • Diagnosis: laparoscopy shows peritubaric adhesions, hydrosalpinx or agglutinated fimbriae; endometrial culture may be positive.
  • Treatment: antibiotics only if culture is positive (doxycycline 100 mg every 12 hours + metronidazole 500 mg every 12 hours for 14 days); pain management with NSAIDs and physiotherapy.
  • Trap: the absence of fever and leukocytosis does not rule out chronic pelvic inflammatory disease.

items.title: Associated irritable bowel syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Associated irritable bowel syndrome

  • Irritable bowel syndrome is associated with chronic pelvic pain in 30-50% of women with chronic pelvic pain; bidirectional relationship due to central sensitization.
  • Recurrent abdominal pain at least 1 day/week in the last 3 months, related to defecation or change in frequency/form of bowel movements (Rome IV criteria).
  • Key difference: the pain of irritable bowel syndrome is relieved by defecation, while gynecological pain is not.
  • First line: soluble fiber (psyllium), antispasmodics (hyoscine 10 mg every 8 hours) and tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline 10-25 mg/night).
  • Trap: in refractory chronic pelvic pain, always rule out irritable bowel syndrome as a comorbidity.

items.title: Urological causes: interstitial cystitis
items.contentHtml:
Urological causes: interstitial cystitis

  • Interstitial cystitis produces suprapubic pain that worsens when filling the bladder and improves when emptying it; urgency and frequency of urination.
  • Diagnosis: O'Leary-Sant questionnaire (score ≥12 suggests interstitial cystitis) and cystoscopy with hydrodistension under anesthesia.
  • Cystoscopic finding: glomeruli (≥10 per field) or Hunner's ulcers (present in 5-10% of cases, classic form).
  • First line: sodium pentosan polysulfate 300 mg/day orally; second line: bladder instillations of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) 50 mL weekly for 6 weeks.
  • Trap: do not perform cystoscopy if active urinary infection; Hydrodistension may worsen pain if Hunner's ulcer is present.

items.title: Pelvic floor dysfunction
items.contentHtml:
Pelvic floor dysfunction

  • Alteration in the coordination, strength or tone of the pelvic floor muscles; causes chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and voiding/defecatory dysfunction.
  • Types: hypertonia (excessive contraction) or hypotonia (weakness); hypertonia is more common in chronic pelvic pain.
  • Diagnosis: manual examination with modified Oxford scale (0-5) for strength and pain on palpation scale (0-10).
  • First line: pelvic floor physiotherapy with biofeedback and relaxation exercises; botulinum toxin A (100-200 U) in trigger points if refractory hypertonia.
  • Trap: pelvic floor dysfunction is often misdiagnosed as endometriosis or irritable bowel syndrome.

items.title: Pudendal neuropathy
items.contentHtml:
Pudendal neuropathy

  • Compression or entrapment of the pudendal nerve (roots S2-S4) as it passes through Alcock's canal; causes pain in the perineal territory.
  • Burning, stabbing or electric pain in the vulva, clitoris, perineum or rectum; worsens when sitting and improves when standing or lying down (saddle sign).
  • Diagnosis: anesthetic Pudendal Nerve Block guided by ultrasound (relief >50% confirms the diagnosis).
  • First line: gabapentin 300-900 mg/day or pregabalin 150-300 mg/day; second line: sacral neuromodulation or surgical decompression.
  • Trap: neuropathic pain does not respond to NSAIDs; suspect if there is a history of traumatic birth or prolonged cycling.

items.title: Evaluation with diagnostic laparoscopy
items.contentHtml:
Evaluation with diagnostic laparoscopy

  • Gold standard for diagnosis of endometriosis and pelvic adhesions; allows biopsy and simultaneous treatment.
  • Indications: chronic pelvic pain >6 months with suspicion of endometriosis, negative image or failure to medical treatment for 3-6 months.
  • Findings: endometriotic implants (red, black, white), adhesions (classification by rASRM score), cysts endometriotic (ovary).
  • Trap: up to 20% of laparoscopies in chronic pelvic pain are normal; does not rule out painful bladder syndrome or pelvic floor dysfunction.
  • Procedure: CO2 insufflation, pressure <15 mmHg, 0° or 30° optics.

items.title: Multimodal and multidisciplinary treatment
items.contentHtml:
Multimodal and multidisciplinary treatment

  • Combined approach: pharmacological (NSAIDs, combined oral contraceptives, gabapentinoids, tricyclic antidepressants), physiotherapy and cognitive-behavioral therapy.
  • Multidisciplinary team: gynecologist, urologist, gastroenterologist, pelvic floor physiotherapist, psychologist/psychiatrist.
  • Analgesic ladder: start with paracetamol 500-1000 mg every 6 hours + ibuprofen 400-600 mg every 8 hours; if no response, add amitriptyline 10-50 mg/night.
  • Interventional procedures: nerve blocks (pudendo, hypogastric), sacral neuromodulation, botulinum toxin A in the pelvic floor.
  • Trap: treatment must be individualized; Surgery (hysterectomy) is not curative if there is a myofascial or neuropathic component.
